comments.text: I am curious about the current state of this problem, is it solved? What exactly is left here, since the problem is still marked open and not on the pending review list on Tao's Wiki.
I have not looked at the claimed (partial) solution yet, for one. Furthermore, if correct, it is not a full solution to the problem, which also asks whether the constant is irrational.
Ah, I see, thank you for the response. Are lean verifications for open problems automatically set to close or do all types of solutions need to be reviewed by someone?
Nothing is automatic, no; nor should it be, since even Lean formalisations need to be checked, to make sure that they are proving the correct statement and compile correctly.
In the AI wiki you can find it in section 1(d) as partial result.
Appreciate it, thank you, just wanted to clear up the confusion between proposed solution and it still being open.

I think I have partial solution to this one: an asymptotic expansion of $f$ and a computable series for the limit. I could not get irrationality. I wrote up the result as a short paper here (arxiv tba). The initial solution was found by GPT 5.4 pro. I then generalized the proof to avoiding other 'forbidden subgraphs' of the divisor graph. Here is the abstract: Erdős asked for the largest size $f(n)$ of a subset of $\{1,\dots,n\}$ with no element dividing two others, and whether $\lim f(n)/n$ is irrational. We show that $f(n)=c_2 n+o(n)$ for an effectively computable constant $c_2$, and moreover that the number $q(n)$ of such subsets satisfies $q(n)=\beta_2^{n+o(n)}$ for a computable constant $\beta_2$. To prove this, we recast the divisibility constraint as forbidding a certain subgraph in the directed divisor graph on $\{1,\dots,n\}$ and prove a more general result: for any finite family of connected forbidden subgraphs of the divisor graph, both the extremal density and counting rate are effectively computable. The proof uses a theorem of McNew on local statistics of divisor graphs. The irrationality of $c_2$ remains open.
Welcome to the site and congrats! I ran standard check which found no issues.
Nice, I have some notes in my files on this problem generated with GPT-5.4 Pro, maybe they will be useful too! Same McNew approach over all.

Just a minor issue. We should use the ceiling function instead of the floor function in the inequality $f(n)\geq \lceil \frac{2n}{3}\rceil$ (by considering the set $\{\lfloor n/3\rfloor+1,...,n\}$). They also use the ceiling function in [Gu04].

I can't access Lebensold's paper, but the Mathscinet review suggests that his method generates more accurate bounds with additional computation - presumably modern computers are good enough so that if someone wants to code up Lebensold's method the first few digits of $f(n)/n$ can be generated and put on OEIS (if not already there).

The digits of the limit might not yet be in OEIS (still having too few reliable digits), but the sequence $n\mapsto f(n)$ is A038372. [Could someone else enter it to the database, I cannot do it at the moment.] (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
